1: midbar Sin (midbār Sîn): the Wilderness of Sin — a geographical name, possibly connected to the Sinai peninsula. The narrator dates the arrival precisely: the fifteenth day of the second month, exactly one month after the Passover (Exodus 12:6). The liturgical precision signals that the wilderness journey is being measured against YHWH's redemptive calendar.
2: yillônû (wayyillônû): 'they grumbled/murmured' — from the root lûn, the technical verb for Israel's wilderness complaint. It appears six times in Exodus 16 alone (Exodus 16:2, 7, 8, 9, 12) and becomes the vocabulary of the wilderness rebellion pattern throughout Numbers. Grumbling is addressed to Moses and Aaron but YHWH hears it as directed at himself (Exodus 16:8).
3: bĕyad YHWH ('by the hand of YHWH'): the wilderness generation prefers death in Egypt under YHWH's judgment to uncertain life in the wilderness. The phrase lĕpōt bāśār ('by pots of flesh') is nostalgic hyperbole. The irony is sharp: they want to die 'by the hand of YHWH' when YHWH's hand has just saved them at the sea (Exodus 14:30-31). The covenant lawsuit begins here: the redeemed people question the goodness of their Redeemer.
4: māṭîr lākem leḥem min-haššāmayim: 'I will rain down bread from heaven for you.' The verb māṭar (to rain) is normally used of rain; here it is used of manna — a deliberate atmospheric metaphor establishing the bread as direct provision from YHWH's domain. The phrase leḥem min-haššāmayim (bread from heaven) is quoted verbatim in Psalms 78:24. The second clause introduces the test motive: lĕmaʿan ʾănassennû hăyēlēk bĕtôrātî ʾim lōʾ — 'in order that I may test them whether they will walk in my Torah or not.' The manna is a structured test of covenant obedience from the beginning.
5: miśneh (mišneh): 'double/twice as much.' On the sixth day the gathering is doubled in anticipation of the seventh day's rest. The same root gives šēnî (second) and šānāh (to repeat). The doubling on the sixth day provides the material basis for Sabbath rest — YHWH's provision anticipates the need before the command is given explicitly.
6: The paired provision announcement: meat (śĕlāw, quail) in the evening and bread (man, manna) in the morning. The evening-morning sequence is the creation rhythm (Gen 1), and the paired provision demonstrates YHWH's complete provision across the full daily cycle. Moses and Aaron make clear that the murmuring is heard by YHWH, not merely directed at the two human leaders.
7: kĕbôd YHWH (kĕbôd Yahweh): 'the glory of YHWH.' The noun kābôd derives from kābed (to be heavy/weighty); glory is the weight or gravitas of God's self-revelation. YHWH's response to complaint is not judgment but demonstration of his character through provision — the provision is itself a glory-display.
8: lōʾ ʿālênû tĕlunnōtêkem kî ʿal YHWH: 'your grumblings are not against us but against YHWH.' The theological analysis of complaint: when Israel murmurs against Moses and Aaron, YHWH is the real target. This framing recurs in 1 Samuel 8:7 and Luke 10:16 ('whoever rejects you rejects me'). Mediators bear the complaint of the people, but YHWH registers it as directed at himself.
9: qirĕbû lipnê YHWH: 'draw near before YHWH.' The verb qārab (to draw near, approach) is the central verb of the sacrificial system — worshipers draw near with offerings; here YHWH himself draws near with provision. The complaint creates the occasion for theophanic encounter.
10: hinnēh kĕbôd YHWH nirʾāh beʿānān: 'the glory of YHWH appeared in the cloud.' The cloud (ʿānān) is the vehicle of divine presence throughout the wilderness narrative. Nirʾāh (Niphal of rāʾāh, to appear/be seen) indicates a controlled, mediated revelation — YHWH makes himself visible but in qualified form, not unmediated glory.
11: YHWH speaks to Moses: the divine word of promise precedes and guarantees the provision. The structure of Exodus 16:11-12 follows the established pattern: YHWH speaks to Moses, Moses relays to Israel, the promised event happens. The chain of mediation (YHWH → Moses → people → event) will be formalized at Sinai but is practiced here in the wilderness.
12: šāmaʿtî ʾet tĕlunnat bĕnê yiśrāʾēl: 'I have heard the murmuring of the children of Israel.' The phrase wîdaʿtem kî ʾănî YHWH ʾĕlōhêkem ('you shall know that I am YHWH your God') is the recognition formula repeated throughout Ezekiel — the purpose of divine acts is theological knowledge. YHWH provides in order to be known, not merely to satisfy appetite.
13: śĕlāw (śĕlāw): quail — a migratory bird (Coturnix coturnix) that crosses Sinai twice yearly; exhausted migrating quail land in large numbers and are easily caught. The Hebrew word appears three times in the Pentateuch (here; Numbers 11:31-32; Psalms 105:40). The provision is naturalistic but timed and organized supernaturally — the ordinary animal is the vehicle of extraordinary provision.
14: ḥasas dāq mĕḥuspar: 'fine flakes, like frost.' The manna is described in two paired adjectives: dāq (thin/fine) and mĕḥuspar (small/flake-like). The vocabulary of smallness — delicate, fine, thin — emphasizes the manna's otherness from ordinary grain. It descends with the dew, associated with the created abundance of morning moisture.
15: man hûʾ: This two-word phrase is the origin of the word 'manna' and is a studied ambiguity. Man in Aramaic/Hebrew means 'what?' — so man hûʾ is 'what is it?' The narrator preserves both meanings simultaneously: the word for the substance is the question about the substance. Moses's explanation ('it is the bread which YHWH has given you to eat') answers the question without removing the wonder. The name 'manna' is carried through Numbers 11:6-9, Psalms 78:24, and the John 6 Bread of Life discourse.
16: ʿōmer (ʿōmer): a dry-measure unit, defined later (v36) as one-tenth of an ephah. This is its first occurrence in Scripture — the unit is only used in the manna narrative. Each person gathers one omer: legulgōlet ('per head,' literally 'per skull') is the counting unit, anticipating the census of Num 1. The provision is democratized — every person receives exactly enough, contrasting with normal food distribution that follows economic and social hierarchies.
17: The gathering proceeds as commanded but the amounts vary. The phenomenon anticipates the leveling of v18: despite differing quantities gathered, each has exactly what they need. The miracle is not identical individual effort but divinely calibrated distribution — the manna economy points toward 2 Corinthians 8:15.
18: hammarbeh lōʾ heydir wĕhammaʿʿîṭ lōʾ ḥiśîr: 'whoever gathered much had no excess, and whoever gathered little had no lack.' Heydir (surplus/excess) comes from yādar (to remain over); ḥiśîr (lack/deficit) from ḥāsar (to lack). Paul quotes this verse in 2 Corinthians 8:15 as the principle behind the Jerusalem collection: the Gentile abundance supplies the Jerusalem lack. The manna economy becomes the pattern for the covenant community's economic ethics.
19: ʾal yôter mimmennû ʾîš ʿad bōqer: 'let no one leave any of it until morning.' The command against hoarding tests trust: leave nothing overnight, gather fresh each day. YHWH trains his people away from anxiety-driven accumulation toward daily dependence — the structural teaching behind the Lord's Prayer's 'give us today our daily bread' (Matthew 6:11).
20: wayyarūm tôlāʿîm wayyibʾaš: 'it bred worms and became foul.' Those who disobeyed and kept the manna overnight found it infested and rotting. The contrast with the Sabbath provision (v24) is pointed: what breeds worms on ordinary days remains pure on the Sabbath. The same physical substance behaves differently according to YHWH's covenant calendar — the natural order is subordinated to his decrees.
21: Daily gathering in the morning until the sun heats the ground, when the manna melts. The provision is neither storable nor indefinitely available — it must be sought each morning in its appointed time. The temporality of the provision enforces the daily rhythm of covenant dependence that YHWH is building into his people's habits.
22: miśneh leḥem: 'double bread' — on the sixth day the provision itself doubles before Moses announces the Sabbath principle (v23). YHWH's provision anticipates his own command. The assembly leaders come to Moses concerned; the explanation that follows is the first Sabbath announcement in the narrative Torah.
23: šabbat šabbātôn qōdeš laYHWH: 'a Sabbath of complete rest, holy to YHWH.' The phrase šabbat šabbātôn is the absolute-state construction with a cognate noun for intensification — 'Sabbath of Sabbaths' — marking superlative rest. This is the first time šabbāt (Sabbath) appears in the Torah narrative, preceding the Sinai legislation. Qōdeš laYHWH (holy to YHWH) indicates the Sabbath belongs to YHWH's sphere. The Sabbath is revealed in the context of manna provision, not first at Sinai.
24: The Sabbath provision is confirmed by miracle: the manna kept overnight for the Sabbath does not rot or breed worms (contrast v20). The physical preservation of the Sabbath portion is YHWH's material attestation of the day's holiness — empirical confirmation of the Sabbath command before it has been formally legislated at Sinai (Exodus 20:8-11).
25: ʾĕkhuluhû hayyôm kî šabbāt hayyôm laYHWH: 'eat it today, for today is a Sabbath to YHWH.' The manna is the occasion for the first Sabbath teaching: the structure of the created week — six days of provision, rest on the seventh — is instantiated in daily wilderness life before the Ten Commandments are given. Creation theology (Genesis 2:2-3) is embedded in covenant provision.
26: Six days of gathering; on the seventh, none is to be found. The provision respects the Sabbath by its absence — YHWH will not give manna on the day set apart for rest. The refusal to provide enforces the Sabbath rest structurally: you cannot gather on the Sabbath even if you try, because there is none to gather.
27: Some go out to gather on the seventh day and find nothing. YHWH's response (v28) — ʿad ʾānāh mēritem, 'how long do you refuse?' — uses the vocabulary of prophetic rebuke. ʿAd ʾānāh (how long?) is the language of Psalms and Prophets addressing chronic disobedience. The Sabbath command is enforced not only by command but by the absence of provision.
28: miṣwōtay wĕtôrōtāy: The paired terms: miṣwôt (commandments, specific directives) and tôrôt (plural of tôrāh, instructions/laws). The use of tôrôt (plural) suggests a broader instruction complex already in view, anticipating the full Sinai legislation. The manna narrative functions as a pre-Sinai probationary period — a training ground for covenant obedience before the law is formally given.
29: rĕʾû kî YHWH nātan lākem haššabbāt: 'See that YHWH has given you the Sabbath.' The Sabbath is framed as a gift (nātan = to give) before it is a law. The command to remain in one's place (šĕbû ʾîš taḥtāyw) on the Sabbath is the first formal Sabbath stipulation in the narrative, anticipating the later prohibitions against kindling fire (Exodus 35:3) and carrying loads (Jeremiah 17:21-22).
30: wayyišbĕtû hāʿām bayyôm haššĕbîʿî: 'the people rested on the seventh day.' The verb šāḇat (from which šabbāt derives) means 'to cease/desist/rest from activity.' The Sabbath rest is cessation modeled on YHWH's own rest after creation (Genesis 2:2-3: wayyišbōt, 'and he rested'). The covenant community's Sabbath participates in the Creator's rest.
31: man: the name is confirmed. zera gad lāban (like coriander seed, white) and ṭaʿmô kĕṣappîḥît bidbāš (its taste like wafers in honey). Two similes: visual (white like coriander seed) and gustatory (sweet like honey). The honey simile evokes the promised land flowing with milk and honey — the manna is a foretaste of the land's abundance. Numbers 11:8 adds that grinding it produced an oil-cake taste.
32: ʾōmer hāmĕlēʾ: 'a full omer.' YHWH commands a jar of manna to be kept (hinnîaḥ) for generations to see ʾet-haleḥem ʾăšer heʾakaltî ʾetkem ('the bread with which I fed you in the wilderness'). The memorial function: the manna jar placed in the ark (Hebrews 9:4) connects this preservation command to the tabernacle's most holy space, making the manna a permanent witness to YHWH's wilderness provision.
33: ṣinnĕṣenet: a jar or pot — a hapax legomenon, the only occurrence of this word in the OT. The object's uniqueness mirrors the uniqueness of its contents. Aaron places it 'before YHWH' (lipnê YHWH), the phrase used for the sanctuary's inner presence. The manna jar's placement anticipates the ark not yet built — an anachronism the narrator resolves by noting (v34) the specific ark to which it refers.
34: Aaron places the manna before hāʿēdut (the Testimony) — a prospective act. The Testimony refers to the stone tablets to be placed in the ark (Exodus 25:16; Exodus 40:20), not yet given at this point in the narrative. The narrator projects forward from the Sinai covenant to explain the placement. The manna jar sits in the ark alongside the covenant documents — provision paired with commandment, grace alongside law.
35: wĕbĕnê yiśrāʾēl ʾākelû ʾet-hammān arbaʿîm šānāh: 'the children of Israel ate the manna forty years.' The manna provision spans the entire wilderness generation, ending only when they eat the produce of Canaan (Joshua 5:12). The forty-year continuity establishes the manna as the wilderness-period bread in toto. ʿAd bôʾām ʾel-qĕṣēh ʾereṣ Kĕnaʿan ('until they came to the edge of the land of Canaan') — provision ends at the boundary of fulfillment.
36: wĕhāʿōmer ʿăśîrît hāʾêpāh hûʾ: 'and the omer is one-tenth of an ephah.' The parenthetical definition closes the manna chapter with a metrological note. An ephah is approximately 22 liters; an omer is thus about 2.2 liters. The precision serves the narrative's concern with exact provision: exactly enough, measured and calibrated, is YHWH's characteristic mode of grace in the wilderness.
